questionText: Let $n$ be an integer. Show that $12 \mid n$ if and only if $3 \mid n$ and $4 \mid n$. Then explain why the analogous statement with the pair $2$ and $6$ fails, i.e. why $(2 \mid n \text{ and } 6 \mid n)$ does not force $12 \mid n$.

steps.description: Name the statements to be linked.
steps.explanation: Proving an 'if and only if' means establishing both $P \Rightarrow Q$ and $Q \Rightarrow P$. Naming the two claims keeps the two halves of the argument organised.

steps.description: Prove the forward direction P implies Q.
steps.explanation: If $n$ is a multiple of $12$ we can write $n=12k$ and regroup the factors. Seeing $n$ as $3$ times an integer and as $4$ times an integer shows both smaller divisibilities at once.
Because $12$ already contains both a factor of $3$ and a factor of $4$, any multiple of $12$ inherits both. The forward implication is immediate.

steps.description: Begin the reverse direction Q implies P.
steps.explanation: The harder direction is to show the two separate divisibilities combine to give divisibility by $12$. We start from both facts and work towards $n=12t$.

steps.description: Use divisibility by 4 to write n explicitly.
steps.explanation: Turning $4 \mid n$ into the equation $n=4m$ gives us something algebraic to substitute into the other condition. This is the standard way to use a divisibility fact.

steps.description: Apply divisibility by 3 together with coprimality.
steps.explanation: Since $3$ divides $4m$ but shares no factor with $4$, it must divide the other factor $m$ (Euclid's lemma). Coprimality of $3$ and $4$ is the crucial ingredient here.

steps.description: Deduce divisibility by 12 and finish the reverse direction.
steps.explanation: Writing $m=3t$ and substituting back gives $n=12t$, so $12 \mid n$. This completes $Q \Rightarrow P$.
Both directions hold, so the two conditions are equivalent. The equivalence works because $3$ and $4$ are coprime, giving $\operatorname{lcm}(3,4)=12$.

steps.description: Set up the analogous statement with 2 and 6.
steps.explanation: We now test whether replacing the pair $3,4$ by $2,6$ still forces divisibility by $12$. A single counterexample would show it does not.
The number $6$ is divisible by both $2$ and $6$, yet it is not divisible by $12$. So the condition $R$ holds while the conclusion fails.

steps.description: Explain why the argument breaks down.
steps.explanation: Here $2$ and $6$ share the factor $2$, so their least common multiple is only $6$, not their product $12$. The overlapping factor is double-counted, which is exactly why divisibility by both forces only divisibility by $6$.

steps.workingLatex: 12 \mid n \Leftrightarrow (3 \mid n \ \text{and}\ 4 \mid n); \quad (2 \mid n \ \text{and}\ 6 \mid n) \not\Rightarrow 12 \mid n
steps.explanation: Splitting a divisor into two factors gives an equivalence only when those factors are coprime. With coprime $3$ and $4$ it works; with the non-coprime $2$ and $6$ it does not.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $12 \mid n \Leftrightarrow (3 \mid n \text{ and } 4 \mid n)$, because $3$ and $4$ are coprime so $\operatorname{lcm}(3,4)=12$. The pair $2,6$ fails: $n=6$ is divisible by $2$ and by $6$ but not by $12$, since $\gcd(2,6)=2$ gives $\operatorname{lcm}(2,6)=6$.
